Why CSS overflow: scroll Shows No Scrollbar on macOS Chrome (vs. Windows)
Anyone who writes CSS has hit this — you add
to an element, open Chrome, no scrollbar. Send it to a coworker and there's a gray scrollbar sitting on theirs. Same Chrome — what gives? This post starts from the
Before getting into the weird behavior, let's revisit what
how an element handles content that exceeds its own width/height
— pretty basic CSS, but it's used everywhere.
that's 200px wide and 100px tall, but you stuff a long line of text or an image wider than 200px into it. Three things could happen visually:
The content just spills out of the container
The content gets clipped — anything past the bounds is gone
A scrollbar shows up and lets the user scroll through it
Default. Content spills out past the container — nothing's done about it.
Content past the bounds is clipped — invisible and unscrollable.
Always reserves space for a scrollbar, whether or not content overflows.
Scrollbar appears only when content actually overflows.
The pair worth calling out is
— they often get confused, and the difference is small.
So why doesn't overflow: scroll show a scrollbar on my machine?
— they only appear while you're actively scrolling, otherwise they're hidden, and they don't take up layout space. Windows uses
Two people opening the same page in the same Chrome version see different visuals — not because Chrome differs, but because the OS underneath does.
On macOS, scrollbar display is controlled by a system setting — System Settings → Appearance → Show scroll bars. Three options:

The default is "Automatically based on mouse or trackpad" — when you're using a trackpad (or the built-in Mac trackpad), it follows the "While scrolling" behavior. Kind of a tongue-twister — in plain English: the scrollbar is invisible most of the time, a translucent thin bar fades in while you actively scroll, then fades out a few seconds after.
Chrome, Safari, and most Mac browsers follow this system setting by default. So even if you write
, the browser still uses Overlay Scrollbar rendering — no permanently visible scrollbar.
How do you confirm this is what's happening? Two steps:
Now the scrollbar shows up and stays visible. That confirms your CSS is fine — the OS setting was controlling what you see.
Why doesn't Windows have this problem?
Because Windows has always used Classic Scrollbars — they take up a fixed strip (around 15–17px) on the right side, always visible, never fading.
So when your coworker opens the same page, the thin gray bar sits there on the right and
looks exactly like the CSS implies. No "where did the scrollbar go?" mystery.

Side note worth knowing — if you develop on Mac but your users are mostly on Windows, the content width you see is actually wider than what your users get (because their scrollbar steals some width). When doing RWD layout work, borrow a coworker's Windows machine and check the layout there, otherwise you might ship something that feels cramped on the right.
How do I make both sides look the same?
If you want every user — regardless of OS — to see a scrollbar (e.g., so Mac users actually realize there's more content to scroll), here are some options.
The most direct approach is the
CSS pseudo-element. Give it an explicit
and Chrome switches that element's scrollbar from Overlay to Classic — always visible.
language-css shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
After that, Mac Chrome finally shows a permanent scrollbar — and since it's now a Classic Scrollbar, it eats some content width, making the layout closer to what Windows users see.
is a non-standard Webkit / Blink property. Chrome, Edge, Safari, and Opera support it; Firefox doesn't.
is also limited — most of the time it still uses the system's Overlay Scrollbar.
Option 2: Use standardized scrollbar-width and scrollbar-color
If you'd rather not use a non-standard property, CSS later standardized scrollbar styling with two W3C properties (CSS Scrollbars Module Level 1):
— corresponding to default thickness, thin, or hidden.
takes two color values — first one is the thumb, second is the track.

Browser support to watch out for: Firefox has supported these since 64. Chromium (Chrome, Edge) only got support from Chrome 121 onward. Safari needed Safari 26.2 for full support. If you still need to support older browsers, keep
styles. So if you write both, mind the cascade.
You might be wondering — doesn't CSS have
to reserve scrollbar space? Wouldn't that solve this more cleanly?
Unfortunately no — it doesn't apply to macOS's Overlay Scrollbars.

was designed to reserve the layout space a scrollbar would need, to stop content from jumping when the scrollbar appears or disappears. But MDN explicitly says: when the browser is using overlay scrollbars,

In short — it solves the "content jumping" problem under Classic Scrollbars, where the bar takes up space. On macOS's default Overlay Scrollbars, there's no space being taken in the first place, so there's nothing to reserve.
So if your goal is "make Mac Chrome always show a scrollbar",
won't get you there — go back to

Back to the "they see it, I don't" scenario from the top — the root cause isn't bad CSS, and it isn't a Chrome bug. It's an OS-level scrollbar mechanism difference. macOS defaults to Overlay, Windows defaults to Classic, so
Now that you know, the next time you hit this you don't have to question your CSS — it's the OS — and you can reach for

You set overflow: scroll, then realize Mac Chrome shows no scrollbar — but your coworker on Windows sees one fine. This post starts with the four overflow values, then explains macOS's Overlay Scrollbar mechanism — why Chrome on macOS doesn't always show a scrollbar even with overflow: scroll. Three fixes: ::-webkit-scrollbar to force it, the newer standard scrollbar-width and scrollbar-color, and flipping macOS's "Show scroll bars" system setting to "Always".
